blocks.title: Let's understand how you think and act

questions.text: It's 9pm and your group project is due tomorrow morning. You realise the team has been building in completely the wrong direction — the work is wasted and nobody has flagged it. You:

options.label: Take charge and redirect everyone myself — tell them exactly what needs to change tonight
Quietly fix my own section and do what I can, hoping the overall thing holds together
Message each person individually, hear what went wrong, and get everyone on the same page
Call an emergency group call and make everyone stop and rethink the whole approach from scratch

questions.text: It's a completely free Saturday — no plans, no obligations. By evening you'd most likely have:

options.label: Spent most of it with friends or family — calling, meeting, hanging out
Done something productive alone — organised, planned, worked on a project
Lost track of time going deep into something that fascinated me
Chilled and recharged — no plans, no pressure, just rest

questions.text: Your marked test paper comes back with a red cross on an answer you're completely certain is correct. You go home and verify it in two different textbooks — you were right. Next class you:

options.label: Raise your hand and politely challenge the teacher with your sources right there in class
Accept the mark and move on — fighting it creates more stress than it's worth
Say nothing out loud, but feel irritated about it for days
Approach the teacher quietly after class and show them the evidence calmly

questions.text: A new student joins your class mid-semester. It's their first day — they don't know anyone and are sitting alone at lunch looking at their phone. You:

options.label: Walk over, introduce myself, and invite them to sit with my group
Make eye contact and smile — wait to see if they seem open before approaching
Tell a more outgoing friend who's better at this kind of thing and ask them to check in
Leave them to settle at their own pace — they'll find their people

questions.text: Exams just ended and you have 7 full days of holiday with zero obligations — no tuition, no plans, nobody expecting anything from you. Your ideal week looks like:

options.label: Packed with people — friends, hangouts, calls, catch-ups every day
Reading, watching documentaries, picking up something new I've been curious about
Mostly alone at home — sleeping in, peace and quiet, no one needing anything
Doing something useful — volunteering, helping at home, or a project that actually matters

questions.text: You're 20 minutes into a 3-hour board exam that counts for 40% of your grade. You hit a question worth 8 marks — and your mind completely blanks. Nothing. You:

options.label: Panic — heart rate spikes, hands tense up, it becomes hard to focus on anything
Circle it, move to the next question, come back when I'm warmer — this is strategy, not defeat
Close my eyes, breathe slowly for 10 seconds, then try rebuilding the answer from first principles
Write whatever partial answer I have, accept I might lose those marks, and focus fully on the rest

questions.text: Your school's annual cultural day is 3 weeks away. The teacher is looking for student volunteers to help organise it — decorations, logistics, performances, coordination. You:

options.label: Put my hand up to be the overall coordinator — I'll own the whole thing
Volunteer for whatever unglamorous task nobody else wants to do
Jump in with ideas for the theme, the look, the vibe — the creative direction
Wait to see if a close friend is involved first before deciding

questions.text: Your teacher gets an urgent call and has to leave. The class is told it's a free period — no instructions, no task, do whatever you want for 45 minutes. You most naturally:

options.label: Turn to whoever's nearby and start a conversation — suddenly the whole class is talking
Pull out a book, notes, or something I've been meaning to get to
Wander to another class or around school to see what's going on
Put my head on the desk, close my eyes, and genuinely enjoy the silence

questions.text: You walk into class and the teacher is already handing out test papers — on a chapter you skipped because it 'wasn't going to be tested.' The whole class is scrambling. You feel:

options.label: Genuinely anxious — this could really mess up my grade
Annoyed at myself, but I'll just write what I know and move on
Curious, almost amused — let's see how much I've actually absorbed without studying
Calm — one surprise test doesn't define my grade or my worth

questions.text: You've just been assigned a 2000-word research project on any topic of your choice, due in exactly 3 weeks. No check-ins, no milestones — just the final submission. You:

options.label: Open a notebook that evening and plan out exactly what I'll do in each of the 3 weeks
Think about the topic for a few days, then start seriously around week 2
Start writing immediately — I figure it out as I go and that works for me
Stress about it quietly for 2 weeks, then stay up late in the final days to rush it out

questions.text: Your two closest friends had a falling-out two weeks ago over something petty that escalated badly. They're barely speaking. Both are separately messaging you to take their side. You:

options.label: Tell both I'll hear them out separately, then try to get them talking again — I want to fix this
Stay completely out of it — I care about both of them but this isn't my conflict to own
Tell each of them honestly what I think, including where they're wrong — even if they're annoyed at me
Decide which one was actually in the wrong and stand with the other — clearly and openly

questions.text: Results are out. You realise you accidentally used outdated data in the group assignment you submitted — and it probably dragged down the marks of all 5 people in your team. Nobody else has figured it out yet. You:

options.label: Message the group immediately and own it — then tell the teacher and ask if anything can be done
Feel genuinely awful about it — it keeps replaying in my head for days
Try to quietly see if there's a way to resubmit or fix it before the team finds out
Accept it as a mistake, note what I'll do differently, and move forward — dwelling helps nobody

questions.text: You're walking past the staffroom and the door is slightly open. You clearly hear a teacher tell another: 'That student is a lost cause — don't waste your energy on them.' You recognise who they're talking about. You:

options.label: Find the student later and check in on them — they don't need to know what I heard, just that someone cares
Report it to the principal or another trusted teacher — this is genuinely not okay
Pretend I didn't hear it — it's not my place to get involved in teacher business
Feel bothered and think about it for days but genuinely not know what to do

questions.text: A relative hands you ₹500 cash at your birthday. You have no immediate needs and no pressure to spend it anywhere. Most likely you:

options.label: Put it straight into savings — I have something I'm working toward
Spend it on something I've been wanting — why wait?
Keep half, spend half — feels like the balanced thing to do
Use it to try something I've never done before — a book, a class, an experience

questions.text: For your English project you have to create anything on the theme 'change.' Halfway through, you get an unusual idea — it could be genuinely brilliant or it could completely miss the mark and cost you marks. You:

options.label: Go for the risky idea — worst case I fail, but at least it'll be something I actually made
Build the safe version first so I have a fallback, then experiment with the risky one
Check with the teacher before committing — I don't want to waste effort on something they'll reject
Stick with the safe version — I'm here for the grade, not to experiment

questions.text: Your school is holding inter-class debates next month. Each class needs speakers who'll argue in public, a researcher who preps all the data, and a coordinator who manages the whole team. You:

options.label: Volunteer to be a speaker — arguing a position in front of people actually energises me
Sign up as the researcher — I'd rather be the one finding the strongest arguments behind the scenes
Agree to participate only because the class needs bodies — I'd rather not
Take on the coordinator role — timing, logistics, keeping the team on track

questions.text: Your friend spent 3 weeks making a short film for the school festival. They ask you to watch it and give honest feedback before they submit. It has real problems — the story is unclear, the audio is bad, the pacing is off. They're excited. You:

options.label: Tell them clearly what's working and what isn't — honest feedback is the most respectful thing I can give
Focus on what's genuinely good and skip the negatives — they've already submitted it in their head
Ask questions — 'What were you going for here?' — and let them talk their way to the problems themselves
Tell them it's great — I can't bring myself to dim their excitement over a school project

questions.text: It's a regular school morning. You wake up, check your phone, and your stomach drops — you've overslept by 45 minutes. First period has already started. You:

options.label: Heart racing, scramble to get ready as fast as possible — I hate being late, it ruins my whole day
Feel annoyed at myself, get dressed quickly, accept I'll take the late mark, and move on
Text a friend to let the teacher know I'm on my way, buy myself a few minutes
Get ready without much fuss — one late morning out of 200 school days isn't a crisis

questions.text: Your class is discussing a topic and most people are nodding along to a view that you genuinely disagree with — you think it's incomplete and you have a different take. You:

options.label: Say what I think, clearly — the class is better when there's a real counter-perspective on the table
Stay quiet — everyone's agreed and starting a debate isn't worth the awkwardness
Phrase it as a question: 'But doesn't that assume...?' — so it doesn't feel like a direct attack
Write my actual view in my notes but keep it to myself — I'll think it through later

questions.text: You have 5 different tasks to get done today — homework, a reply to an email, a personal project, some reading, and a chore. No deadline conflicts, no specific order required. You typically:

options.label: Rank them by importance and start with the hardest — I want the heavy stuff off my plate first
Start with something easy to get going, then work up to the harder ones
Work on whichever one I feel pulled toward in the moment — I flow between tasks naturally
Get stuck deciding where to start and end up losing time before I actually begin

questions.text: During a group presentation, your classmate presents an idea you privately suggested to them last week — and takes full credit for it in front of the teacher without mentioning you. You:

options.label: Correct it right there: 'Actually, that was something I had suggested...' — calmly but clearly
Say nothing now, but bring it up with them privately after class
Let it go — the idea got used, I know where it came from, that's enough for me
Say nothing but feel genuinely resentful about it for days

questions.text: For your history project you can choose your own topic. Option A: the Mughal Empire — you've studied it all year and know it well. Option B: the Byzantine Empire — genuinely interesting to you, but you know almost nothing about it. You:

options.label: Pick Byzantine — the fact that I know nothing about it is exactly what makes it interesting
Stick with Mughal — I know it, I'll do it better, I want the marks
Pick Byzantine but give myself extra time to research thoroughly before writing anything
Ask the teacher which one is likely to score higher before deciding

questions.text: You've just had a draining school day — back-to-back classes, a stressful test, and a lot of people. You get home at 4pm with a free evening ahead. What actually recharges you?

options.label: Calling a friend or heading out to meet someone — connecting with people lifts me up
Going to my room, putting on headphones, and having zero people around for a few hours
Something physical — shooting hoops, going for a run, even a long walk
Getting absorbed in something — a book, a series, a game — just me and the thing

questions.text: Your school announces that phones will be completely banned on campus — even during lunch and free periods — starting next month. You think it's excessive and unfair. You:

options.label: Start organising — get classmates to sign a petition and present it to the principal as a group
Follow the new rule without making a fuss — it's their school, not mine
Vent to friends about how unfair it is, but don't actually do anything
Request a meeting with the teacher or principal and make my case respectfully with reasons

questions.text: Your principal asks you personally to deliver the opening address at the school's annual day — 800 students and parents in the auditorium, 5-minute speech, on stage. How do you genuinely feel?

options.label: Excited — I'll take any platform I can get, and this one's a good one
Nervous, but I'll write the speech carefully and prepare until I feel ready
Dreading it — public speaking in front of that many people is genuinely distressing for me
Fine — it's a bit uncomfortable but it's part of school life and I'll just do it

blocks.title: What kind of work excites you?

questions.text: If you had to pick one thing to spend a Saturday afternoon on, what would it be?

options.label: Build or fix something with your hands
Research a mystery or unsolved question
Create art, music, or write a story
Organise a get-together or help a friend
Try to sell something or pitch a business idea
Organise your room, plan your week, make lists

questions.text: You're on YouTube. Which video do you click first?

options.label: How a jet engine actually works — engineering explained
Why humans dream — science of the brain
How this 19-year-old made ₹10 lakh from scratch
A spoken word poem that made millions cry
Documentary: people who rebuild lives after disaster
The most efficient way to organise any workspace

questions.text: School gives you a free period — no rules, work on anything. You pick:

options.label: Write a short film script or comic strip
Organise a fundraiser for a cause you care about
Analyse some data or facts you've been curious about
Fix or make something physical — a prop, a model
Map out a detailed plan for something big in your future
Teach a concept you love to a younger student

questions.text: Which school subject do you actually look forward to the most?

options.label: Physics / Chemistry / Biology
Maths
English / Literature / Languages
History / Geography / Social Studies
Business Studies / Economics
Art / Music / Drama

questions.text: You're on a school trip to a new city. You're most excited to:

options.label: Visit a factory or see how industrial things are made
Go to a science museum or planetarium
Wander local markets and chat with vendors about their trade
Explore street art, live music, or local theatre
Visit an NGO or community centre doing real work
Tour the city's civic infrastructure — courts, city hall, hospitals

questions.text: Your dream project is something that:

options.label: Solves a real physical problem — clean water, safer structures
Discovers something nobody has known before
Makes people feel something powerful — a film, a song
Directly improves the lives of real people in need
Builds a company or brand that changes an industry
Creates a perfectly designed system others depend on

questions.text: A friend group is starting a new project together. Which role do you naturally end up in?

options.label: Building or making the actual product
Doing the research — digging up what we need to know
Coming up with the look, feel, and creative direction
Keeping the group motivated and resolving tension
Taking charge of the strategy and making key decisions
Managing the timeline, tasks, and making sure nothing falls through

questions.text: If you had to spend 2 hours helping someone, which would you enjoy most?

options.label: Helping them fix or build something broken
Helping them understand a concept that's confusing them
Helping them express their feelings creatively
Just being there, listening, and giving advice
Helping them figure out a business or money problem
Helping them get organised or manage their time

questions.text: Which of these would you most like to be known for at 30?

options.label: Building something impressive with my own hands
Being an expert who knows more than almost anyone in my field
Creating something beautiful that people still talk about
Being someone who genuinely helped a lot of people
Building a successful business or leading a major team
Running a well-oiled operation that never drops the ball

questions.text: You're most productive when you're:

options.label: Working with tools, equipment, or physical materials
Digging into a complex problem with no clear answer
Making something from scratch with creative freedom
Collaborating closely with people I care about
Leading a team toward a big goal under some pressure
Following a clear process and checking things off a list

questions.text: You get an unexpected free afternoon. Without thinking, you'd probably:

options.label: Tinker with something — fix a bike, try cooking, make something
Go down a rabbit hole reading about something I've been curious about
Sketch, write, play an instrument, or watch a great film
Call someone who might need company or a listening ear
Try to hustle — sell something, plan a project, explore an opportunity
Tidy up, reorganise, or plan the week ahead

questions.text: Which kind of class activity do you enjoy most?

options.label: Lab sessions where we do hands-on experiments
Case studies or problems with no single right answer
Creative assignments — essays, art projects, performances
Group discussions where we hear different perspectives
Competitions or debates where I can persuade people
Structured tasks where I follow clear steps and get results

questions.text: When you imagine a really satisfying workday at age 25, it looks like:

options.label: I built or fixed something that now actually works
I figured out something that was confusing everyone else
I made something that didn't exist before and it's beautiful
Someone told me I genuinely made a difference to them today
I closed a deal or convinced a room full of people to believe in my idea
I ran a complex operation flawlessly without a single error

questions.text: At a family gathering, a relative asks what kind of work you want to do. The most honest answer is:

options.label: Something where I'm building or making real things
Research or analysis — I want to deeply understand something
Something creative — I want to make art or tell stories
Working with people — teaching, counselling, healthcare
Running my own thing or leading a big team
Something stable with clear processes — finance, administration, law

questions.text: If you could shadow someone for a week, whose job would you pick?

options.label: A structural engineer building a bridge
A research scientist running experiments
A film director or graphic novelist
A therapist or social worker
A startup founder or top business negotiator
A chartered accountant or court administrator

questions.text: When a big problem needs solving, you're most likely to:

options.label: Try different physical approaches until something works
Research every angle before doing anything
Think of an unexpected creative angle nobody considered
Get people together and solve it collaboratively
Take charge and push through decisively
Break it into steps and work through them one by one

questions.text: You're most uncomfortable when you have to:

options.label: Sit at a desk for hours without doing anything physical
Make decisions without enough information or data
Follow strict rules that leave zero room for creativity
Work completely alone with no human contact for days
Take orders from someone whose judgment I don't trust
Work without any clear structure, deadlines, or defined goals

questions.text: Which of these sounds most like a fun challenge to you?

options.label: Build a working object using only basic materials
Solve a puzzle that requires pattern recognition and logic
Express a complex emotion through a visual or written piece
Help someone through a hard decision or personal crisis
Convince a skeptical audience to believe in an idea
Audit a process for inefficiencies and fix every single one

questions.text: The best kind of homework for you would be:

options.label: A practical task — build a model, do an experiment
Research an open-ended question and form your own view
Create something original — a poem, short film, illustration
Interview someone in the community about their experience
Present a business or proposal to the class
Complete structured exercises with correct/incorrect answers

questions.text: Which of these news headlines would make you read more?

options.label: Engineers build bridge using only recycled materials
Scientists finally explain why we age — and how to slow it
This 22-year-old's graphic novel won the Booker Prize
Local teacher changes 50 kids' lives through just one lesson
Student turns ₹2000 into a 6-figure business in 18 months
Bank's new fraud detection system catches 99.8% of cases

questions.text: How much can your family invest in college per year (roughly)?

options.label: Under ₹1 lakh
₹1–5 lakh
₹5–15 lakh
₹15 lakh+
We'd consider an education loan for the right college

blocks.title: What do you want your life to look like?

questions.text: Close your eyes for a second. It's 2041. You're 30 years old, established in your career. Where are you living — and is that a choice or a compromise?

options.label: A big metro — Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore. I want the energy, the opportunity, the scale
A growing mid-size city — enough career options, less chaos, actually liveable
Back in my hometown — I want to build something there, not just leave
Wherever the best work is — I'm not attached to a place, I'm attached to what I'm doing

questions.text: If you could design your own career from scratch — forget what's practical for a second — what would it absolutely have to give you? Pick the one thing you'd refuse to compromise on:

options.label: Stability — I need to know what's coming in every month, no roller coasters
High income — I want real financial freedom, not just a decent salary
Meaning — I need to feel like the work actually matters, not just to me
Autonomy — I want to decide how, when, and where I work

questions.text: You have ₹5,000 saved up over several months. A friend offers you a deal: flip it into a bet — 50% chance you walk away with ₹15,000, 50% chance you lose all ₹5,000. The alternative is leaving it in a fixed deposit at guaranteed 7% interest. You have no immediate need for the money. You:

options.label: Fixed deposit, no question — I worked for that ₹5,000 and I'm not gambling it
Take the bet — expected value is positive and I can handle losing it if I have to
Think it through carefully — I'd want to know more about the bet before deciding either way

questions.text: By 2035, AI will likely be doing jobs that humans do today — coding, writing, diagnosis, legal research. You'll be 24. When you think about this honestly, you feel:

options.label: Worried — I want to build a career that's genuinely hard for AI to replace
Excited — I want to work with AI and use it as a tool to get ahead, not compete with it
Unconcerned — every generation faces disruption and people always find their way

questions.text: It's 2037 and you're 28. You love your job. Describe a typical Tuesday — not the highlights, just a regular good day at work. Which version sounds most like you?

options.label: Back-to-back — meetings, presentations, calls, negotiating with people all day. I thrive on the interaction
Long stretches of deep focus on a hard problem — headphones in, no interruptions, fully absorbed
A mix — some focused solo work in the morning, some collaboration in the afternoon
Out in the field — visiting places, meeting people on-site, always moving around

questions.text: Two job offers: Job A pays ₹30 LPA and is genuinely meaningful — you're working on something that helps people. Job B pays ₹50 LPA and is completely fine but doesn't particularly matter to the world. Both have similar hours and growth. You take:

options.label: Job A — I can't spend 10 hours a day on something that means nothing to me
Job B — the extra ₹20 LPA is real and I'll find meaning in other parts of my life
Hard to say — I'd want to talk to people in both roles before deciding
Job A, but mainly because low-stress sustainable work tends to be in mission-driven places

questions.text: You're 26. Three years into a stable, well-paying job with a reputable company. Good salary, no major stress. But you dread Mondays and feel like you're slowly fading. You:

options.label: Push through — I've built something stable here and excitement is overrated in a career
Start quietly planning an exit — save more aggressively and look for something that excites me
Try to change what I can from the inside first — request new projects, take on more, find the interesting parts
Resign when I have 3 months of savings — figure out the next move with a clear head

questions.text: At 30, you have full control over your schedule. You can choose how much you work — but the more you work, the more you earn and build. The less you work, the more time you have. What do you actually choose?

options.label: 60–70 hours a week — I'm trying to build something and this is the season for it
40–45 hours — I want to do great work and still have evenings and weekends
Depends on the month — I'd rather have peaks and valleys than a rigid routine
As little as I can sustain — I'd genuinely rather earn less and have more of my time back

questions.text: At 29, one of these is your reality. Which would genuinely bother you the most day-to-day — not which sounds worst in theory, but which would actually eat at you?

options.label: I'm earning significantly less than most of my friends doing similar work
I'm good at my job but I don't believe in what my company actually does
I have no control over my time — the company owns my schedule completely
My role feels like it could be cut or automated at any point — no security

questions.text: After 12th, your parents sit you down and make their case: pursue CA or engineering — not because you love it, but because it's a safe, high-paying path and they've seen what instability does. You:

options.label: Agree — their lived experience is real data and I'd rather be secure than right
Respectfully disagree — prepare a proper counter-argument with research and present it to them
Feel genuinely torn — their concerns are valid but so is mine, and I'm not sure what to do
Take their path but treat it as a 3–5 year plan — earn, save, then transition to what I actually want

questions.text: A friend who started a business 2 years ago calls you excited — it's finally profitable. But they also worked 70-hour weeks, had 6 months with no income, and nearly quit three times. When you hear that story, your honest reaction is:

options.label: That sounds incredible — the hard parts are part of what makes it worth it
I admire it, but I'm not sure I could handle 6 months of no income
Genuinely not for me — I'd rather master a craft inside a great organisation than own the whole thing
Maybe — if I had the right idea and the right co-founder, I could see it

questions.text: It's 3am and you're lying awake thinking about your future. Which of these is most likely what you're actually anxious about — not the most noble-sounding one, but the real one?

options.label: Not earning enough to take care of my parents and give them the life they deserve
Spending 40 years doing something that doesn't change anything or matter to anyone
Making the wrong call on which career to pursue and being trapped in it
Looking back at 45 and realising I traded all my best years for a career and nothing else
